video_title: The Chokepoints Changing Global Shipping Forever with Sal Mercogliano | TGS 227
gist: Maritime historian Sal Mercogliano tells Nate Hagens that Iran's mining and quasi-legal claims over the Strait of Hormuz are being managed less by international law than by insurance markets and missing US security escorts, part of a broader post-WWII-first fracturing of freedom of the seas across the Persian Gulf, Red Sea, and Black Sea. The conversation argues this is driving a structurally higher cost floor for oil and shipping, accelerating China's dominance of shipbuilding with deliberately shorter-lived vessels, and pushing Russia toward Arctic trade routes as sanctions-evading 'dark fleets' proliferate.

themes.theme: Strait of Hormuz: Contested Control and Legal Ambiguity

insights.claim: Iran and Oman effectively split legal control of the Strait of Hormuz down the middle since the strait is only 21 miles wide and territorial waters extend 12 nautical miles from each shore, but international transit-passage rights under UNCLOS are supposed to guarantee free commercial passage regardless of whose territorial waters a ship crosses.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Legal ambiguity means enforcement, not treaty text, will determine whether tankers can transit; watch for unilateral Iranian permitting moves as the real signal.
insights.quote: the straits 21 miles wide, I mean, it's kind of owned by Iran and Oman, kind of split down the middle

insights.claim: Neither Iran nor the United States is a signatory to UNCLOS, which weakens the legal footing either side has to invoke the treaty in the Hormuz standoff.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Legal disputes over transit rights will likely be settled by military and insurance leverage rather than treaty arbitration.
insights.quote: Iran is not a signatory to unclause. And by the way, neither is the United States.

insights.claim: Iran has created a 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority,' an entity with no real international legal standing, to project an appearance of legitimate control over Hormuz shipping permits.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Watch whether Oman formally joins this authority, which would give Iran a much stronger practical claim to control or toll the strait.
insights.quote: They've created this entity called the Persian Gulf Strait Authority which gives them kind of the air of legitimacy which it really isn't.

insights.claim: Iran has mined the central traffic-separation shipping lanes of the Strait of Hormuz, confirmed by both Iranian and US statements, with the IMO identifying roughly 80 mines believed laid in the region.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Minefields create de facto chokepoint risk even without confirmed strikes, since the mere threat of mines is enough to disrupt transit.
insights.quote: Problem with minefields is you actually need zero mines to create a minefield. You just need the threat of mines

insights.claim: A ceasefire or diplomatic resolution between the US, Israel, and Iran is unlikely to quickly restore Hormuz shipping volumes, because commercial shipowners hedge against even a small chance of catastrophic loss in a way militaries do not.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Expect shipping costs and insurance premiums through Hormuz to stay elevated well after any political settlement, a structural rather than cyclical repricing.
insights.quote: They're going to sit there and go, "Oo, how do I hedge that loss?"

insights.claim: After the September 2025 Gaza-Houthi ceasefire, commercial shipping did not return to the Red Sea despite the declared truce, because owners judged the underlying risk of renewed attack unchanged.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: A purely diplomatic resolution in Hormuz is unlikely to be sufficient to bring shipping volumes and rates back to pre-crisis levels; expect a Red-Sea-style lag.
insights.quote: not going to attack ships anymore. But commercial shipping didn't come back.

themes.theme: Insurance and Security, Not Diplomacy, Are the Real Chokepoint Mechanism

insights.claim: War-risk insurance premiums for transiting the Persian Gulf jumped from about 0.15% of vessel value pre-crisis to 1-5% during the crisis, still below the 5-10% rates seen during the 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq Tanker War.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Track P&I club war-risk rate filings as a leading indicator of perceived Hormuz risk; a move toward Tanker War-era 5-10% would signal serious sustained escalation.
insights.quote: you were talking at about maybe a quarter of a percent of insurance or maybe.15%. Now you're talking about 1 to 5%.

insights.claim: It was not insurance availability but the absence of US-provided security escort that actually halted Hormuz transits; shipowners obtained higher-cost war-risk coverage and were ready to sail, but held ships in place because adequate protection wasn't in place.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Resumption of Hormuz traffic hinges on US or allied naval escort commitments, not just insurance market capacity.
insights.quote: one of the things one of the narratives you'll hear is it was insurance that stopped everything from happening. And that's not true

insights.claim: Twelve international P&I (Protection & Indemnity) clubs, owned collectively by shipowners, insure roughly 90% of the world's commercial shipping fleet, making them a de facto private governance layer over global maritime risk.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: These clubs, not governments, are the practical enforcement mechanism behind sanctions regimes and war-risk access; their decisions matter as much as government sanctions.

insights.claim: Standard shipping insurance covers vessel, cargo, and crew replacement but not the 10-15 years of future income lost from a scrapped ship, which takes 3-4 years to replace, so owners weigh a roughly $1 million war-risk premium against a much larger uninsured earnings loss when deciding whether to sail into a conflict zone.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Explains why some owners refuse to sail even when insurance is available and affordable; the real deterrent is uninsurable long-run earnings loss, not the premium itself.
insights.quote: you're not getting the replacement of that ship over the next life of that ship, 10 to 15 years

insights.claim: Some Greek shipowners were offered up to $750,000 per day to run tanker cargoes out of the Gulf during the crisis, roughly seven to fifteen times the normal charter rate of $50,000-$100,000 per day.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Elevated spot charter rates for Gulf-transiting tankers are a direct, tradeable signal of ongoing risk premium in the tanker market.
insights.quote: maybe you're making $50 to $100,000 per day for that vessel.

insights.claim: The Iranian-created Persian Gulf Strait Authority is offering shipowners 'free' war-risk insurance that only covers hostile acts Iran itself has committed, and after 60 days it can begin levying transit fees; if Oman (which controls the strait's southern half) formally co-signs, Iran would gain a much stronger practical claim to toll the strait, similar to precedents at the Turkish and Danish Straits.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Watch Iran-Oman negotiations closely; a joint toll regime would layer a structural, possibly durable new cost onto all Hormuz transits.
insights.quote: they're providing you war risk insurance against a list of things, which is all the things Iran has done, by the way.

themes.theme: Redundancy, Resilience and the Cost of Just-in-Time Supply Chains

insights.claim: Firms with pre-built redundant shipping routes, such as importers already routing goods via the Panama Canal or through Charleston, Savannah, New Orleans, and Houston instead of relying solely on LA/Long Beach, adapted quickly when the Houthis began attacking Red Sea shipping in late 2023 and 2024, while firms fully committed to a single route could not.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Route and port diversification is a measurable resilience premium for shippers and importers, not just a sunk cost.

insights.claim: China deliberately built redundancy into its energy and trade system over the past two decades, including strategic petroleum reserves and alternate suppliers, which is likely why the common narrative that a Hormuz shutdown would hurt China the most is overstated.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Discounts the consensus bearish-China-from-Hormuz-shock trade; China's energy security position may be structurally stronger than commentary assumes.

insights.claim: Building resilience into supply chains is inherently costly, and because business cycles are short-term, firms tend to strip that resilience back out again within a few years once memory of the shock fades.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Don't assume today's Hormuz-driven diversification is permanent; the resilience premium trade likely fades over a multi-year horizon.
insights.quote: The problem is this we all know in the business cycle is very short term because once this is caked into the system within a few years people are going to go I need to cut funds

insights.claim: The 2021 global supply-chain crisis, when 109 ships waited off LA/Long Beach at once, was not a shortage of ships but a shortage of onshore infrastructure such as drayage capacity, Class I rail, and warehousing.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Onshore infrastructure bottlenecks, not vessel supply, are the recurring binding constraint in supply-chain crises, a useful diagnostic for the next disruption.
insights.quote: We have plenty of ships. The issue isn't the ships. The problem is that a lot of people

insights.claim: Fertilizer disruption from the Persian Gulf conflict, compounding ongoing Black Sea supply issues from the Russia-Ukraine war, will likely affect the next planting season in Eritrea, West Africa, India, and Australia even if the Hormuz conflict ends soon, because the impact of rerouted urea and ammonia phosphate shipments only shows up at harvest.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Agricultural commodity investors should watch fertilizer freight and pricing data now as a leading indicator for next season's crop input costs.

themes.theme: Fragmenting Freedom of the Seas and the Rise of the Dark Fleet

insights.claim: For the first time since the end of World War II, freedom of the seas is being meaningfully challenged simultaneously in the Persian Gulf (Iran), the Red Sea (Houthis), and the Black Sea (Russia-Ukraine), representing a structural bifurcation of the world's oceans rather than isolated regional conflicts.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: This is the macro thesis of the episode: treat elevated freight and insurance costs as a durable new regime, not a transient shock.
insights.quote: We're seeing really freedom of the seas challenged in the first meaningful way since the world wars.

insights.claim: A sanctions-evasion 'dark fleet' or 'parallel fleet' emerged from Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine to carry sanctioned oil, initially Venezuelan and Iranian and then Russian, above the G7 price cap, using self-created insurance and flags of convenience registered in countries like Mongolia and Madagascar, with some vessels sailing entirely unregistered and stateless.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: This fleet represents uninsured tail risk that governments increasingly seize when found in their waters, an escalating enforcement trend to watch.
insights.quote: we're talking about like Mongolia and Madagascar

insights.claim: The G7/EU oil price-cap sanctions strategy against Russia, enforced by having the 12 major P&I insurance clubs refuse to insure cargo priced above roughly $60 per barrel, was substantially circumvented by Russia building its own shadow insurance and flag-registration system.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Insurance-based sanctions enforcement has a demonstrated workaround; expect similar circumvention if used against other sanctioned trade, such as Iranian oil.

insights.claim: Uninspected dark-fleet tankers pose escalating safety risk: one ship-to-ship transfer vessel exploded off Malaysia, killing two crew, because its inert-gas explosion-prevention system had failed and would have been caught by a port inspection the ship was deliberately avoiding.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Rising dark-fleet tonnage raises the tail risk of a major uninsured environmental spill, a liability that would fall on the coastal state.

insights.claim: The global merchant fleet is aging faster than it is being replaced, with the average vessel now about 23 years old and getting older each year even as demand for shipping capacity increases.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: A structurally tightening supply of compliant, insurable tonnage supports higher long-run charter rates independent of any single geopolitical event.
insights.quote: Ships are getting older. Uh the ships on average now are about 23 years old and they're getting older by the year.

insights.claim: China has the world's #2 navy and #2 merchant marine, giving it more comprehensive maritime leverage than the US, which has the #1 navy but ranks only around #23 in merchant marine size.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: US naval primacy does not translate into commercial maritime leverage; expect renewed US policy pushes to rebuild a national merchant fleet.
insights.quote: it's very hard to be a sea power when you have the number one navy in the world than the number 23 merchant marine in the world.

themes.theme: China's Shipbuilding Dominance and Deliberately Shrinking Vessel Lifespans

insights.claim: China's share of global commercial ship orders rose from about 5% in 1999 to 70.9% in 2025, with the state-owned China State Shipbuilding Corporation (CSSC) alone building roughly 40% of the world's ships, more than any other country besides China itself.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: China's near-monopoly on new vessel construction gives it long-run leverage over global fleet composition, standards, and pricing.
insights.quote: latest report has China in 2025 with 70.9% of all commercial orders for ships

insights.claim: Global shipyard order backlogs are now so long that a ship ordered today would not be delivered until 2029-2030, even though construction itself only takes about 2-3 years.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Any near-term surge in shipping demand cannot be met with new tonnage for years, a structural supply constraint supporting elevated charter rates.
insights.quote: orders are so pushed back right now that if you order a ship right now, you're not going to get it to 2029, 2030.

insights.claim: Ships are increasingly designed for shorter operational lifespans of roughly 15-20 years, down from 30-40 years historically, partly due to uncertainty from IMO net-zero-by-2050 emissions rules and, the guest argues, because a shorter design life suits China's shipbuilding-dominance strategy by guaranteeing a continuous replacement-order cycle.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Expect structurally higher new-build order volumes over time even without demand growth, benefiting Chinese shipbuilders disproportionately, plus rising ship-recycling activity in India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh.
insights.quote: they want a short lifespan for a ship. They want 15 to 20 years

insights.claim: The Trump administration has pushed back against the IMO's net-zero-by-2050 framework, arguing it disproportionately benefits China's shipbuilding position and imposes excessive cost through new fuel infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Watch for continued US regulatory divergence from IMO emissions rules, a live policy fight with implications for shipbuilding investment and fuel-infrastructure capex.

insights.claim: Ammonia is emerging as a leading candidate for carbon-free marine fuel, but it is highly toxic and can be lethal to crew if it leaks, prompting the creation of dedicated 'green corridor' shipping routes designed around its safe handling.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Ammonia-fuel infrastructure buildout is a distinct investable theme within maritime decarbonization, separate from LNG or methanol.
insights.quote: Ammonia is is carbonf free. It's it's fantastic. However, carbon leaks are really excit

themes.theme: Arctic Routes Reshaping Global Trade Geography

insights.claim: Russia's Northern Sea Route (Arctic/Northeast Passage) carried over 30 million tons of cargo last year, its highest volume ever, and this year saw its earliest-ever Arctic passage by an ice-strengthened LNG carrier, as Russia seeks a route that avoids all contested chokepoints and Mediterranean attack risk.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Arctic shipping capacity is becoming a genuine strategic alternative for Russia; watch Russian icebreaker fleet investment and Arctic LNG carrier orders as a hedge against Mediterranean and European sanctions risk.
insights.quote: we saw last year the highest amount of throughput through the Arctic Ocean by the Russians, over 30 million tons.

insights.claim: A Russian LNG carrier, the Arctic Metagas, was struck by two Ukrainian unmanned surface vessels in the Mediterranean and nearly sank, making Russia reluctant to route LNG exports through the Med and pushing it toward the longer but safer Arctic route to reach Asian buyers.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Ukrainian USV strikes on Russian energy shipping are a live tail-risk factor reshaping Russian LNG export routing and effectively taxing Russian energy trade with longer voyages.
insights.quote: we had an example of one of their ships, the Arctic Metagas, being struck by two unmanned surface vessels and very nearly sinking the vessel.

insights.claim: Europe's planned January 2027 cutoff of Russian LNG imports is pushing Russia to find alternative buyers, reinforcing its incentive to develop the Arctic route to Asia even though it is a longer voyage than shipping to Europe.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Watch whether the 2027 EU Russian-LNG ban actually holds; if it does, expect further build-out of Russia's Arctic LNG shipping capacity and closer Russia-Asia energy integration.

insights.claim: The Panama Canal's throughput is constrained by freshwater levels in Gatun Lake, and a forecast 'super El Nino' this summer is already causing preemptive transit restrictions similar to the 2023-2024 drought, when daily transits were cut from 32-36 ships to 22.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Panama Canal capacity, already absorbing diverted Hormuz-related traffic, faces a second, independent supply constraint from climate-driven drought, compounding chokepoint risk this year.
insights.quote: when you get below 85 86 ft, then you don't have enough water to run the canal efficiently

insights.claim: The Northwest Passage through Canadian Arctic waters is not yet commercially viable at scale because it isn't ice-free year-round, is narrower and more constrained than the Russian route, and doesn't meaningfully cut transit distance versus existing routes like the Panama Canal, though it could become more relevant as more seas grow contested.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Don't overweight near-term Northwest Passage trade-route plays; the economic case remains marginal versus existing infrastructure for now.

themes.theme: Macro Read-Through: A New, Higher Cost Floor for Oil, Shipping and Trade

insights.claim: The host argues the Hormuz disruption is likely to set a new, higher structural floor for oil prices, oil product prices, and shipping costs generally, independent of near-term political resolution, because damaged supply chains and trust are slow and costly to rebuild.
insights.speaker: Nate Hagens
insights.so_what: Position for a persistently higher cost baseline across energy and freight rather than a snap-back to pre-crisis pricing once headlines fade.
insights.quote: I think this is going to put a new floor on oil prices globally generally.

insights.claim: Despite the US-Iran/Israel conflict, oil markets showed no appreciable drop in overall shipping activity because commercial actors anticipated the risk in advance and began stockpiling oil at sea at the highest levels since COVID, a response that likely cushioned the broader economy.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Elevated floating oil storage is a real-time gauge of anticipated supply disruption; monitor tanker-tracking stockpile data as a leading indicator during future Hormuz flare-ups.
insights.quote: we started to see was stockpiling of oil at sea. Uh literally the highest level since CO

insights.claim: Only about 20-25% of global trade currently runs through the actively disrupted chokepoints of Hormuz, the Red Sea, and the Black Sea, while roughly 75% of global trade continues to flow normally.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Size the Hormuz/Red Sea/Black Sea shock as a targeted ~20-25% of trade impact rather than an economy-wide trade stoppage, while watching for further spillover.
insights.quote: that's impacting maybe 20 to 25% of global trade. The other 75 is moving along very very well.

insights.claim: Even under tariffs as high as 145% on Chinese goods, many US importers continued sourcing from China because it remained the cheapest option and, in many cases, the only source capable of matching their required production specifications.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Tariff-driven decoupling from China is likely slower and shallower than headline policy suggests; watch actual import volume data rather than tariff announcements to gauge real reshoring progress.
insights.quote: even when we put 145% tariffs on goods coming from China. Some importers were telling me, it's like, I'm still going to go to China.

insights.claim: Bulk commodity trade, such as iron ore, lithium, and copper, moves more total tonnage than oil or containers, exemplified by Port Hedland, Australia, which ships about 750 million tons of raw iron ore per year, roughly 5% of all global shipping tonnage, largely unrefined at the point of export.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Bulk raw-material shipping capacity, not container or tanker capacity, is the largest single component of global maritime tonnage and a key structural demand driver for the bulk-carrier fleet.
insights.quote: they load 750 million tons a year. That is 5% of the global tonnage.

insights.claim: At least 14 merchant mariners have been killed since the Hormuz conflict began, 11 by Iranian action and 3 by a US strike that hit a commercial ship in the Gulf of Oman, with roughly 20,000 sailors stranded in the Persian Gulf at the crisis's peak facing freshwater shortages, food shortages, and blocked crew rotations due to grounded flights.
insights.speaker: Sal Mercogliano
insights.so_what: Humanitarian and operational strain on crews is a hidden cost multiplier likely to further raise wage and retention costs across the merchant marine workforce.
insights.quote: grand total 14 merchant mariners have been killed since the start of this conflict. 11 by the Iranians and three unfortunately by the US
